questions.specification:
A coach of a school basketball team introduces a programme based on self-determination theory to improve athletes’ motivation and well-being. The coach provides frequent performance feedback, uses team-building activities to strengthen relationships, avoids controlling language, and tries to create a supportive team climate.

With reference to this scenario, answer the following questions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.2 Self-determination > C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b)-(d) focus on competence and relatedness needs, feedback supporting competence, and controlling coaching reducing autonomous motivation—core elements of self-determination theory (autonomy, competence, relatedness).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.3 Motivational climate > C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks about how a supportive climate contributes to psychological well-being, aligning with motivational climate (mastery/supportive environments) effects on well-being and behavior.

parts.content: Outline how feedback contributes to competence development.
parts.markscheme:
- Highlights progress and areas for improvement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reinforces mastery and perceptions of success <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases confidence and willingness to persist/try again <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps athletes reflect, set goals and self-regulate performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides specific information on how to correct errors/what to do next <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can improve perceived competence by confirming skill improvement over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how relatedness influences behavior in a team sport.
parts.markscheme:
- Strengthens team bonds and trust, increasing willingness to work for teammates <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages cooperative/prosocial behavior (eg, passing, supporting, helping) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Builds loyalty and mutual accountability to team goals and norms <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces interpersonal conflict by increasing empathy and respect <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances communication and cohesion, improving coordinated team play <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases motivation to attend training/compete due to feeling accepted and valued <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can reduce antisocial behaviors (eg, blaming, exclusion) because group identity is stronger <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the negative effects of controlling coaching behavior on athlete motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces autonomy, lowering intrinsic motivation/interest in the sport <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shifts motivation towards controlled forms (external pressure, guilt), reducing self-determination <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increases stress/anxiety due to fear of punishment, criticism or failure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can reduce effort/persistence and increase dropout/burnout risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Diminishes athlete–coach trust/relationship quality, reducing engagement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Undermines creativity/decision-making because athletes avoid taking initiative <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lowers perceived competence/confidence if feedback is overly critical or conditional <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotes fear-based compliance rather than learning and mastery focus <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how a supportive climate contributes to psychological well-being.
parts.markscheme:
- Encourages emotional safety and a sense of belonging <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces fear of failure by normalizing mistakes as part of learning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances self-esteem/self-worth through respect and positive regard <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotes open communication and help-seeking when stressed or injured <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports sustained engagement and satisfaction/enjoyment in participation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can reduce negative affect (eg, anxiety) by lowering perceived pressure and threat <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages adaptive coping and resilience through social support <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A coach notices one athlete thrives when competing against others, while another thrives when improving personal skills. This difference is best explained by:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario contrasts thriving on outperforming others (ego orientation) vs thriving on personal improvement/mastery (task orientation), which is the core distinction in goal orientation theory.

options.content: Self-determination theory
Goal orientation theory
Attribution theory
Drive reduction theory

questions.specification: According to goal orientation theory, why does a task-oriented athlete tend to have higher resilience?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks, within goal orientation theory, why task-oriented athletes show higher resilience—directly targeting task vs. ego orientation and how success is defined (self-improvement vs comparison).

options.content: Because success is defined by self-improvement rather than comparison with others
Because they ignore emotional factors
Because they persist through setbacks by viewing effort and learning as part of progress
Because they avoid challenging goals

questions.specification: Which factor in Need Achievement Theory directly shapes behavioural outcomes?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need Achievement Theory is an achievement motivation framework emphasizing how personality and situational (environmental) factors influence behavioural outcomes (approach/avoidance tendencies).

options.content: Emotional factors
options.markscheme: In Need Achievement Theory, emotional reactions (specifically the focus on pride of success versus the shame of failure) are the factors that directly influence the final achievement behaviour (the outcome).

options.content: Genetic inheritance
options.markscheme: Need Achievement Theory is a social-cognitive model focusing on motives and situational factors, not biological or genetic inheritance.

options.content: Environmental distraction
options.markscheme: While environmental factors may affect performance, 'environmental distraction' is not a component of Need Achievement Theory.

options.content: Physical fitness
options.markscheme: Physical fitness is a physiological state and is not included as a factor in the Need Achievement Theory psychological model.

questions.specification: Which of the following scenarios illustrates ego orientation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions

options.content: A cyclist is motivated by beating their training partner’s time
A gymnast aims to improve technique regardless of score
A runner strives to learn a new breathing rhythm
A swimmer focuses on mastering a new stroke

questions.specification: According to Achievement Goal Theory, which type of goal orientation is focused on self-improvement and personal mastery?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which goal orientation in Achievement Goal Theory focuses on self-improvement and personal mastery, which directly maps to task vs. ego goal orientation (Goal orientation theory).

options.content: Ego orientation
Task orientation
options.markscheme: Correct: task (mastery) orientation emphasizes self-referenced improvement and learning.

options.content: Performance (ability) orientation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: performance/ability orientation focuses on demonstrating superior ability relative to others (normative comparison).

options.content: Mastery-avoidance orientation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: mastery-avoidance focuses on avoiding doing worse than one has done before, rather than striving for mastery/self-improvement.

questions.specification: Which of the following is most likely in an athlete with strong task orientation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about characteristics of an athlete with strong task orientation (e.g., viewing failure as learning), which is directly covered by goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions.

options.content: They are motivated mainly by outperforming others
They see failure as a learning opportunity
They experience low intrinsic motivation
They avoid challenging situations

questions.specification: According to goal orientation theory, what is the main reason athletes participate in sport?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks, per goal orientation theory, why athletes participate in sport—this directly targets task vs. ego orientation and the desire to feel successful/define success.

options.content: To demonstrate competence and feel successful
options.markscheme: Correct: goal orientation theory proposes participation is driven by perceptions of competence/success (task or ego involvement).

options.content: To avoid anxiety
options.markscheme: Incorrect: anxiety avoidance is more closely linked to avoidance motivation/anxiety theories than goal orientation theory’s competence-based aims.

options.content: To observe and imitate elite performers
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this reflects social learning/modeling rather than goal orientation theory.

options.content: To achieve a personal best through mastery of a skill
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this is a task/mastery focus, but the question asks for the main general reason for participation in goal orientation theory (competence/success overall), not a specific orientation.

questions.specification: A football player bases confidence only on outperforming teammates. This ego orientation is most likely to lead to:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts ego orientation (outperforming teammates) with outcomes like anxiety/loss of confidence, directly reflecting goal orientation theory (task vs ego).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically asks about consequences of relying on ego orientation for confidence; this matches the HL focus on challenges/problems associated with high ego orientation.

options.content: More consistent motivation
Anxiety and loss of confidence when not the best
Greater focus on personal mastery
Increased intrinsic motivation

parts.content: Explain how task-oriented and ego-oriented athletes differ in their motivational approach.
parts.markscheme:
- State that task-oriented athletes focus on personal improvement, learning, and mastery of a skill <marks>A1</marks>
- State that ego-oriented athletes compare their performance to others to demonstrate superior ability or gain validation <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that task orientation is associated with higher levels of persistence, resilience, and consistent effort <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that ego orientation may lead to performance anxiety, reduced effort when failure is likely, or athlete dropout <marks>A1</marks>
- Discuss that task-oriented motivation is often intrinsic, whereas ego-oriented athletes may rely on external rewards and validation <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Describe the role of the three basic psychological needs in self-determination theory.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe autonomy as a sense of choice and control over one's actions, which increases engagement and self-direction <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe competence as the belief in one's ability to perform a task or interact effectively with the environment, which is essential for confidence <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe relatedness as a sense of social connection, belonging, and feeling cared for by others, which enhances team cohesion <marks>A1</marks>
- State that satisfying these three needs supports the development of intrinsic motivation and sustained effort <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that deficits in any of these three needs can lead to demotivation and higher rates of athlete dropout <marks>A1</marks>
- Conclude that satisfying all three needs simultaneously leads to optimal psychological functioning and well-being <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Discuss how a motivational climate can influence athlete behavior and long-term participation.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that a task-oriented (mastery) climate fosters a focus on learning, cooperation among teammates, and persistence <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that an ego-oriented (performance) climate encourages social comparison, heightened stress, and a fear of failure <marks>A1</marks>
- State that a positive, mastery-oriented motivational climate is linked to higher levels of athlete retention and enjoyment in the sport <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that the motivational climate shapes an athlete's mindset and determines their ability to use effective coping strategies during adversity <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of autonomy-supportive coaching on athlete development.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that autonomy-supportive coaching promotes athlete autonomy and involves them in decision-making processes <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that it enhances intrinsic motivation and creates a sense of ownership over personal progress and goals <marks>A1</marks>
- State that it helps build a relationship of trust and psychological safety between the athlete and the coach <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that it encourages exploration and experimentation, leading to long-term commitment and passion for the sport <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify as a limitation that it requires consistent, individualized implementation and may be difficult for coaches accustomed to autocratic or controlling styles <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

The tendency to strive for success and meet personal standards

questions.specification: Why does need-achievement theory link personality to situational context?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need achievement theory explains achievement motivation as arising from the interaction between personality dispositions (e.g., need to achieve/avoid failure) and situational factors (e.g., perceived probability and incentive value of success), matching the idea that motivation results from their interaction rather than either alone.

options.content: Because motivation results from the interaction between personality and the situation, rather than either factor alone.
options.markscheme: Correct: need-achievement theory proposes behaviour/motivation is influenced by both personal dispositions and situational factors in combination.

options.content: Because situational factors generally have a greater influence than personality on achievement motivation.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: need-achievement theory does not claim situational factors are generally more important than personality; it emphasises their interaction.

options.content: Because situational context can influence how strongly a person’s need for achievement is expressed.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this may be true, but it omits the role of personality and does not capture the interaction between personality and situation as the key link.

options.content: Because personality traits can shape how an athlete interprets and responds to achievement situations.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this may be true, but it omits the role of situational context and does not capture the interaction between personality and situation as the key link.

questions.specification: Which is the clearest example of task orientation in athletics?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the clearest example of task orientation versus ego/outcome focus; this is directly covered by goal orientation theory (task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions).

options.content: A sprinter focusing on improving start technique regardless of winning
A tennis player motivated only by defeating rivals
A gymnast competing only for medals
A footballer giving up when behind on the scoreboard

questions.specification: In Atkinson’s need achievement theory, which statement correctly describes the role of resultant tendencies?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need Achievement Theory describes achievement motivation as arising from the interaction of personality factors (e.g., need to achieve/avoid failure) and situational factors, with resultant tendencies producing emotional responses that influence behaviour—matching the question’s focus on the theory’s chain.

options.content: Resultant factors produce emotional responses that influence behaviour.
options.markscheme: Correct: In Atkinson’s model, approach/avoidance (resultant) tendencies influence behaviour and are associated with affective responses.

options.content: Achievement motivation depends only on winning.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: The theory includes motives (e.g., need to achieve/avoid failure) and expectancy/incentive components, not only winning.

options.content: Incentive value is determined only by situational factors and is independent of probability of success.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Incentive value and probability of success are related within the model and both contribute to resultant tendencies.

options.content: High probability of success always produces a stronger tendency to approach than moderate probability of success.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: In the model, approach tendency is typically strongest at moderate probability of success when combined with incentive value and the motive to achieve.

questions.specification: According to achievement motivation (need achievement) theory, which statement is most accurate?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need achievement theory in sport psychology explains achievement motivation as arising from the interaction of personality factors (e.g., need to achieve/avoid failure) and situational factors, matching the option about personality and situation combining to influence behaviour.

options.content: Achievement behaviour is influenced by the motive to achieve success and the motive to avoid failure, together with the perceived probability and incentive value of success.
options.markscheme: Correct: describes need achievement theory (nAch/nAf) and the role of probability/value of success.

options.content: Success in sport is determined only by genetics.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: need achievement theory focuses on motivational motives and task-related perceptions, not genetic determinism.

options.content: Motivation is unrelated to the environment.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the theory includes situational/task factors such as probability of success and incentive value.

options.content: Performance always stays the same regardless of task difficulty.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the theory predicts changes in achievement behaviour with task difficulty and perceived chances of success.

parts.content: (d) Explain how intrinsic and extrinsic motivation differ in their effect on performance.
parts.markscheme:

- Explain how extrinsic motivation is driven by external factors such as rewards, recognition, or social pressure <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain how intrinsic motivation is generally more sustainable for long-term participation and performance adherence <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: (e) Outline the consequences of low motivation in sport participation.

4 marks total

<note>Other acceptable answers include poor team cohesion, lack of goal setting, or reduced enjoyment.</note>

- Discuss the encouragement of the setting of individual performance and process goals <marks>A1</marks>

- Discuss the creation of a supportive, non-comparative training environment <marks>A1</marks>
- Discuss the recognition and reward of individual improvement and persistence <marks>A1</marks>

parts.markscheme:

- higher likelihood of sport dropout <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept other valid consequences such as reduced performance quality or low engagement.</note>

parts.content: Describe the influence of autonomy on motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- autonomy increases athlete engagement and interest in the activity <marks>A1</marks>
- encourages self-direction and taking responsibility for one's own actions <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain how competence contributes to the development of intrinsic motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- mastery of skills increases an athlete's confidence and self-efficacy <marks>A1</marks>
- perceived competence encourages continued participation and effort as the athlete feels capable <marks>A1</marks>
- competence enhances feelings of self-worth and personal accomplishment <marks>A1</marks>
- achievement of personal goals reinforces the internal drive to continue improving <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline how a coach's leadership style impacts motivational climate.
parts.markscheme:
- democratic leadership supports athlete autonomy by involving them in decision-making processes <marks>A1</marks>
- autocratic styles may reduce intrinsic drive by utilizing controlling behaviors and focusing only on outcomes <marks>A1</marks>
- supportive leadership styles build athlete trust and increase overall motivation levels <marks>A1</marks>
- the coach's behavior determines whether the climate is mastery-oriented (focus on improvement) or performance-oriented (focus on winning) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss how self-determination theory can be used to design effective sport environments.
parts.markscheme:
- address the three basic psychological needs: autonomy (choice), competence (skill mastery), and relatedness (connection) <marks>A1</marks>
- provide meaningful, informational feedback that focuses on improvement rather than controlling feedback <marks>A1</marks>
- offer opportunities for collaborative goal-setting and providing choice in training methods <marks>A1</marks>
- avoid the use of controlling language or excessive reliance on external rewards that can undermine intrinsic drive <marks>A1</marks>
- design supportive environments that foster a sense of belonging, safety, and community among athletes <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification:
Which athlete is most associated with *fragile motivation* (high ego orientation, low task orientation), increasing the risk of performance anxiety under pressure?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/r1z2IsFJX-OAX8MgnubKi.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The diagram/options describe task vs ego orientation profiles and asks who is most at risk of anxiety/dropout under pressure—core application of goal orientation theory.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The highest-risk option is characterized by high ego and low task orientation with fear of judgement; this aligns with the HL leaf on challenges associated with high ego orientation (e.g., vulnerability under failure/pressure).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.1 Arousal and anxiety > C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question links the motivational profile to performance anxiety under pressure, drawing on how anxiety can negatively affect performance in competitive settings.

options.content: A sprinter with high ego orientation but low task orientation who fears being judged by peers after poor trials
options.markscheme: Correct (A): high ego + low task is labelled as fragile motivation in the figure, which is linked to anxiety under pressure.

options.content: A swimmer with high task orientation and low ego orientation who focuses on process goals regardless of outcome
options.markscheme: Incorrect: high task + low ego corresponds to persistent motivation in the figure, not fragile motivation.

options.content: A basketball player with low ego orientation and low task orientation who enters competitions casually and infrequently
options.markscheme: Incorrect: low task + low ego corresponds to no motivation (amotivation) in the figure, not the fragile-motivation (high ego + low task) profile linked to performance anxiety under pressure.

options.content: A tennis player with high task orientation and high ego orientation who trains with both internal goals and social drive
options.markscheme: Incorrect: high task + high ego corresponds to high performance motivation in the figure, not fragile motivation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions

options.content: It reduces emphasis on learning and effort when the athlete is not winning.
It increases anxiety and fear of failure, which can reduce persistence after setbacks.
It encourages avoiding challenging tasks to protect perceived ability, limiting improvement.

questions.specification: Which of the following best illustrates the **emotional reactions** component of need achievement theory?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need achievement theory is part of achievement motivation; the question targets the theory’s components (emotional reactions such as pride/shame after success/failure), aligning with how personality/situational factors shape achievement motivation.

options.content: A footballer feels pride after scoring a goal.
options.markscheme: In need achievement theory, emotional reactions refer to the pride or shame experienced following success or failure. High achievers typically focus on the pride of success, which influences their future achievement behavior.

options.content: A gymnast evaluates the probability of success before a routine.
options.markscheme: Evaluating the probability of success is a situational factor, not an emotional reaction.

options.content: A runner chooses to compete against opponents of a similar skill level.
options.markscheme: This illustrates a resultant tendency, specifically the tendency of high achievers to seek out tasks with a 50% chance of success (challenges).

options.content: A swimmer is primarily driven by a high motive to avoid failure.
options.markscheme: The motive to avoid failure is a personality factor, not an emotional reaction.

questions.specification: Which scenario best illustrates the interaction of personality and situational factors in Need Achievement Theory?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need Achievement Theory is an achievement motivation theory emphasizing how personality (e.g., competitiveness/need to achieve) interacts with situational factors (e.g., evaluative audience, competitive setting) to influence behavior and performance.

options.content: A sprinter with high competitiveness thrives when racing in front of a large crowd
options.markscheme: Need Achievement Theory is an interactionist model that considers both personality factors (the motive to achieve success and the motive to avoid failure) and situational factors (the probability of success and the incentive value of success). This scenario illustrates a personality trait (competitiveness) interacting with a situational context (crowd/competition).

options.content: A swimmer improves by practicing more often
A gymnast learns new skills by modeling elite athletes
A marathon runner maintains a steady pace using attentional control

questions.specification: Why can high ego orientation be problematic if task orientation is low?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts ego vs task orientation and asks why high ego orientation is problematic when task orientation is low—core of goal orientation theory.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically targets the downsides of high ego orientation (e.g., focusing on outperforming others rather than self-improvement), which aligns with challenges to motivation from high ego orientation.

options.content: Athletes may avoid challenging tasks to reduce the risk of failure when they cannot outperform others.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a plausible negative outcome of high ego orientation with low task orientation, but it is not the best match to the stem compared with option C.

options.content: Athletes may rely too much on skill transfer.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Skill transfer is not a key consequence of high ego orientation with low task orientation in achievement goal theory.

options.content: Athletes may focus only on outperforming others rather than improving themselves.
options.markscheme: Correct. High ego orientation emphasizes normative comparison (beating others); without task orientation (mastery/improvement), this can lead to maladaptive motivation focused on winning rather than self-referenced improvement.

options.content: Athletes may show reduced persistence and effort when they are losing or not the best.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a plausible maladaptive outcome of high ego orientation with low task orientation, but it is less direct than option C in answering why it can be problematic.

questions.specification: Which example best reflects Need Achievement Theory in sport?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need Achievement Theory (achievement motivation) emphasizes motivation as an interaction between personality traits (e.g., motive to achieve/succeed, fear of failure) and situational factors; the tennis player example explicitly describes motivation influenced by both personality and competition setting.

options.content: A runner avoids training due to poor weather conditions.
options.markscheme: This reflects an external, unstable attribution (Attribution Theory) rather than Need Achievement Theory.

options.content: A tennis player’s motivation is influenced by both their personality and the competition setting.
options.markscheme: Need Achievement Theory (McClelland-Atkinson model) is an interactional view that considers personal factors (motives) and situational factors (probability and incentive value of success) to predict achievement behavior.

options.content: A cyclist chooses events only based on the perceived difficulty of the course, ignoring their own motives for success or fear of failure.
options.markscheme: This overemphasizes task difficulty (a situational factor) and neglects the interaction with personal motives central to Need Achievement Theory (motive to achieve success vs motive to avoid failure).

options.content: A swimmer’s success is determined solely by genetic traits.
options.markscheme: This reflects a trait-centered or biological view of behavior, whereas Need Achievement Theory is an interactionist model.

questions.specification: Athletes with high task and high ego orientation are likely to:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly references goal orientation theory and balanced task and ego orientations, which is directly covered by task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions.

options.content: Have both competitive drive and self-improvement goals
Prioritize self-improvement while caring little about outperforming others
Focus only on external validation
Lose motivation quickly after setbacks

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.3 Motivational climate > C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) focus directly on creating a task-oriented (mastery) climate and explaining how motivational climate influences persistence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) discusses ego orientation and its effects on goal setting and performance, which is core to goal orientation theory (task vs ego).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.3 High ego orientation and its potential challenges to motivation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) specifically targets impacts of high ego orientation, aligning with the HL leaf on challenges to motivation from high ego orientation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (d) and (e) involve goal setting (including intrinsic goal-setting strategies) as a motivation tool, matching goal setting content.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.2 Self-determination > C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) refers to intrinsic strategies to enhance motivation, which links to self-determination theory and supporting autonomy/competence/relatedness.

parts.content: Outline ways a coach can foster a task-oriented climate.
parts.markscheme:
- Emphasise effort over outcome <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide individualised, process-based feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourage peer collaboration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reward mastery and learning <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how motivational climate affects athlete persistence.
parts.markscheme:
- Task climates support long-term motivation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Athletes persist despite setbacks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces burnout and dropout <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fosters intrinsic interest <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reinforces value of effort <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the impact of ego orientation on goal setting and performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Ego-oriented athletes set performance-based goals <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May avoid challenging situations to protect image <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High ego orientation leads to stress under pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Success is measured against others <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can result in inconsistent performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Often undermines intrinsic motivation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how a coach can use intrinsic goal-setting strategies to enhance motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Involve athletes in setting personal goals <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Focus on skill development rather than external rewards <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Set goals based on progress and learning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide autonomy-supportive rationale <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monitor and adapt goals collaboratively <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: An athlete motivated by setting personal bests in training is showing:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.2 Goal orientation theory: task- vs. ego-oriented success perceptions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts ego vs task orientation; being motivated by setting personal bests reflects task (mastery) orientation as described in goal orientation theory.

options.content: Ego orientation
Task orientation
Resultant orientation
Situational orientation

questions.specification: According to need achievement theory, behavioural outcomes result from the interaction of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.1 Achievement motivation > C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1.1 Interaction of personality and situational factors in determining motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Need achievement theory explains achievement motivation and performance as arising from the interaction between personality factors (e.g., need to achieve/avoid failure) and situational factors (e.g., probability and incentive value of success).

options.content: Personality and genetic factors
Situational and personality factors
Environmental and biological drives
Training and fitness levels

questions.specification: This question assesses understanding of the Self-Determination Theory (SDT) and its application in sport psychology, focusing on how psychological needs influence intrinsic motivation and long-term engagement.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.2 Self-determination > C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.2.1 Self-determination theory and the basic needs of autonomy, competence, and relatedness
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) explicitly target SDT basic psychological needs (competence and autonomy), and part (d) requires integrating all three needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness) to improve motivation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.3 Motivational climate > C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.3.1 Motivational climate impacts performance through mastery and ego approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (d) and (e) discuss how coaches create an intrinsic motivational climate and the long-term benefits of that climate, aligning with motivational climate (mastery vs ego) impacts on motivation and engagement.

parts.content: Outline how perceived competence influences intrinsic motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Define perceived competence as the belief in one's ability to master a skill or task <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain how high perceived competence increases confidence and self-efficacy <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how it increases an individual's willingness to engage in and persist with challenges <marks>A1</marks>
- State that mastery of these challenges reinforces a sense of accomplishment and internal drive <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Explain the role of autonomy in the Self-Determination Theory.
parts.markscheme:
- Define autonomy as the need for individuals to feel in control of their own behaviors and goals <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain how autonomy promotes ownership of an athlete's training and performance <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how providing choices encourages creativity and persistence within the sport <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that higher autonomy reduces pressure-driven stress and external control <marks>A1</marks>
- State that autonomy supports long-term engagement and adherence to an exercise program <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Discuss how coaches can integrate all three psychological needs to improve motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how coaches can support autonomy by allowing athlete input on drills and strategy <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline how competence can be supported by setting achievable goals and providing constructive feedback <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain how relatedness is fostered through supportive team relationships and social bonding <marks>A1</marks>
- Discuss how meeting all three needs leads to optimal, self-determined motivation <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe the resulting increase in athlete resilience and enjoyment of the activity <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain how this integration reduces the athlete's reliance on extrinsic motivators or rewards <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Describe the long-term benefits of an intrinsic motivational climate.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain how intrinsic motivation promotes consistent and voluntary participation <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe the improvement in athlete well-being, satisfaction, and self-esteem <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify the reduction in athlete burnout and performance-related anxiety <marks>A1</marks>
- Outline how it builds a sustainable internal drive for excellence and mastery <marks>A1</marks>
- State how it fosters a positive relationship with sport, supporting lifelong physical activity <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total
